participants.name: Kenji (8ptzsm)
participants.professional_persona: Kenji is a postdoctoral fellow at A*STAR working on generative AI for drug discovery. He moved to Singapore from Japan to collaborate on a joint grant call between Singapore and Japan.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in molecular graph generation, protein-ligand docking prediction, and high-throughput screening automation. Has published in Nature Machine Intelligence.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to establish a biotech AI startup in Singapore that partners with local hospitals for clinical trials. Aims to contribute to Singapore’s reputation as a hub for AI-driven life sciences.

participants.name: A (rilnrf)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-rilnrfeg
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-rilnrfeg
participants.professional_persona: A former preschool teacher, I retrained into AI via a Career Conversion Programme. I now work in a public agency’s Smart Nation team, building AI tools that help social workers identify at-risk families earlier.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I specialise in natural language processing for multilingual text analysis and have developed a chatbot that answers citizens’ queries on community services. I also advocate for inclusive AI design.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to champion the use of AI for social good, particularly to support vulnerable groups. My longer-term ambition is to design AI literacy programmes for non-tech public officers.

participants.name: An (hz5tnw)
participants.professional_persona: An AI safety and ethics specialist who ensures responsible AI deployment in a multinational corporation. She contributes to the development of trusted AI guidelines.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep understanding of AI fairness, bias detection, and model interpretability. Experienced in designing governance frameworks and conducting audits.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to shape Singapore's responsible AI standards and become a leading voice in the global AI ethics community.

participants.name: A (ocgqbg)
participants.professional_persona: A computer vision engineer at an industrial automation startup. He previously participated in an AI Trailblazers programme and now builds AI solutions for manufacturing.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in deep learning for image recognition and edge AI deployment. Skilled in rapid prototyping and working with limited compute resources.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to develop resource-efficient AI models that can run on low-power devices, making AI accessible to small factories.

participants.name: Priya (2ta59r)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-2ta59rl1
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-2ta59rl1
participants.professional_persona: Priya is a mid-career data scientist at a local fintech startup, passionate about making AI accessible for underbanked communities. She actively participates in industry hackathons and incubator programmes to identify pain points where AI can drive inclusion.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in machine learning, natural language processing, and Python. Experienced in deploying AI prototypes for financial inclusion use cases.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: She aims to lead a social-impact AI team within an enterprise and eventually advise the government on ethical AI adoption for vulnerable groups.

initial_posts.narrative: As a data scientist focused on AI for public good, I see the government's commitment of over S$500 million as a strong signal to support R&D and adoption. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated and aligns with enabling enterprises to tackle meaningful AI problems without prohibitive costs. For compute, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on the need to support multiple experiments and the scale of typical cloud allocations for such programs. For trainers, I estimate 150 personnel, drawing from my experience in hackathons where a ratio of about 1 trainer per 3-4 projects is effective, and given the target of 100 experiments, this seems reasonable to provide adequate guidance.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so I take these as ground truth.

Compute infrastructure is a key enabler; based on typical cloud GPU allocations for national AI programs (e.g., 1000 GPU hours per experiment for 500 experiments), 500,000 GPU hours supports a robust pipeline.
Trainer count of 150 is derived from needing to support multiple concurrent projects and trainees, with a ratio of 1 trainer per 2-3 projects for effective mentorship, as seen in similar programs.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group focused on how to translate S$500 million funding and 150 trainers into activities that drive 100E project approvals and deployments. There was strong consensus that platform onboarding assists should match the 100-experiment target, with all participants estimating exactly 100. For other activities, estimates converged around moderate numbers: enterprise outreach sessions ranged from 40 to 50 (central tendency ~45), researcher matchmaking events from 15 to 20 (~18), and training workshops from 25 to 30 (~28). Disagreements centered on the pace of engagement, with some favoring more aggressive outreach and matchmaking to maximize volume, while others emphasized quality interactions and careful pairing to ensure viable problem statements and successful matches.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 150 trainers, I estimate that 80 out of the targeted 100 experiments will be approved in 2023, as the rigorous selection process for viable problem statements and the need for quality researcher-enterprise pairings will filter out some proposals. From these, I expect 60 AI prototypes to be successfully built, reflecting the technical challenges and iterative nature of AI development. The AI Apprenticeship Programme, with 28 training workshops and 150 trainers, should produce 120 trainees completing the programme, assuming an average of 4-5 trainees per workshop and a 90% completion rate. Finally, I estimate 70 enterprises will have at least one AI solution developed through the programme, as some prototypes may not reach a deployable state or may be integrated into existing products without being counted as a separate solution. My estimates are slightly conservative compared to the policy's ambition, reflecting my experience in AI ethics where quality and responsible deployment often slow down initial outputs.
evidence: The 100E programme's co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project and the need for viable problem statements mean not all 100 slots will be filled in the first year; 80 approvals is a realistic target based on typical pipeline attrition.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme and AI Apprenticeship Programme would yield gradual, steady progress across all four impact metrics. All participants estimated Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 6th, AI sector GDP share at 3.8%, public AI trust at 72%, and AI talent retention at 85%, with no variation in numeric predictions. Key causal mechanisms included strong government infrastructure and talent development driving readiness, deployed 100E projects and generative AI use cases contributing to GDP, responsible AI practices boosting trust, and hands-on training retaining talent. However, participants consistently noted that competition from larger economies, early-stage scaling of solutions, lingering concerns about job displacement and bias, and lures from global tech giants prevented higher scores. No disagreements emerged, as all narratives and evidence aligned closely.
